notes.note: "Our friend" is Pompey, named here by bitter euphemism; the disgrace Cicero foresees is his failure to march to the relief of Domitius, besieged at Corfinium. Cicero predicts (rightly) that Pompey will abandon Domitius rather than risk battle.

A cohort was a tenth of a legion, roughly 480 men, so thirty cohorts is about three legions; Cicero stresses that Pompey had ample force to rescue Domitius and chose not to. The reproach measures Pompey's nerve against his means.

notes.anchor: I would rather be conquered with Pompey than conquer with those men
notes.note: Cicero's own much-quoted formula for his dilemma, which Atticus had praised: he prefers defeat in Pompey's cause to victory in Caesar's. He now qualifies it bitterly --- it held for the Pompey who once was, not for the man now fleeing Italy.

Pompey's decision to evacuate Italy and cross to Greece, abandoning Rome and the homeland to Caesar, is what Cicero cannot forgive; it transforms the war from a defence of the state into flight. This crossing (from Brundisium, March 49) is the hinge of the whole book.

Moneta is the temple of Juno Moneta on the Capitol, where the Roman mint stood; "from Moneta" means drawing on the public treasury or coined money. Cicero adds "for nobody is paying" --- in the chaos of war ordinary credit has dried up.

notes.anchor: those Oppii who lodge with you
notes.note: The Oppii were banker-brothers of Velia (on the Lucanian coast) who shared lodgings with, or did business alongside, Atticus; Cicero looks to them as an alternative source of travel funds. They recur in the correspondence as a financial channel during the war.
